q: 109 Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight. Four square conducting plates, all having the same side length but different thicknesses, are positioned so that they are parallel to one another, as shown in Figure 1 . Plates 1 and 4 have negligible thickness, and plates 2 and 3 have the same thickness D . Figure 1 is not drawn to scale in that the side length of the plates is much greater than both D and the distances between the plates. Figure 2 shows a side view of the plates and indicates the plates’ positions along the x -axis of a coordinate system. Plates 1 , 2 , and 3 have net charges of + Q 0 , - 3 Q 0 , and zero, respectively, where Q 0 is a positive quantity. Plate 4 is connected to ground by a wire. Also shown in Figure 2 are three labeled points A , B , and C . Points A and C are each located midway between adjacent plates, and Point B is located inside Plate 3 at the plate’s center. (a) In Figure 3 , draw arrows to indicate the direction and magnitude of the electric field, if any, at each of the labelled points. If the field is zero at any of the points, write “ E = 0 ” next to the appropriate dot. Otherwise, represent the field direction at each point with an arrow that starts on, and points away from, the dot. Note: The relative lengths of the arrows should reflect the relative magnitudes of the fields at the different points. Next, all four plates are connected to ground and a small hole is drilled through the center of each plate. All four plates are then disconnected from ground and repositioned as in Figure 4 . Charge is then added to the plates, resulting in electric fields between adjacent pairs of plates with directions and magnitudes as indicated in Figure 4 . Assume that the holes do not affect the electric field. A charged particle is released from rest immediately to the right of the hole in Plate 1 . The particle has mass m and a positive charge + q . Initially, the particle accelerates to the right, toward the hole in Plate 2 , due to the electric field between plates 1 and 2 . The particle passes between plates 2 and 3 into the region between plates 3 and 4 . The particle either comes to an instantaneous stop before reaching the hole in Plate 4 , OR reaches the hole in Plate 4 . (b) As appropriate, derive an expression for either the distance from Plate 3 at which the particle comes to an instantaneous stop OR the kinetic energy of the particle when it reaches the hole in Plate 4 . Express your answer in terms of D , E 0 , m , q , and physical constants, as appropriate. (c) On the axes in Figure 5 , sketch a graph of the particle’s speed as a function of position x in the region 0 < x < 4 D , which covers both the region between plates 1 and 2 and the region inside the hole of Plate 2 . (d) Indicate whether the graph you drew in part (c) is consistent or inconsistent with the electric field between plates 1 and 2 shown in Figure 4 . Justify your claim that the graph and the electric field are either consistent or not consistent.

q: 108 Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight. A variable capacitor consists of two semicircular, parallel plates of the same size, as shown in Figure 1 . One plate is fixed in place, while the other plate can be rotated through various angular positions, resulting in a varying amount of overlapping plate area. The capacitance is determined by the amount of overlapping area and the distance between the plates. Also indicated in the figure is the angle θ between the straight edges of the plates. The capacitor plates have been given equal-but-opposite amounts of charge, and a group of students wishes to experimentally determine the amount of charge on the plates. The students have an assortment of standard measurement equipment that includes a voltmeter probe, shown in Figure 2 . The voltmeter probe can measure the potential difference between two conducting surfaces by touching one of the measurement tips to each conductor. Assume that using the voltmeter does not significantly affect the charge distribution or the amount of charge on the plates. Also assume the net charge on each plate resides uniformly only on the areas where they overlap. (a) i. Indicate quantities the students could measure that would allow them to determine the charge Q on one of the capacitor plates by using a linear graph. ii. Briefly describe a method to reduce experimental uncertainty. (b) i. Indicate which quantities the students could graph on the horizontal and vertical axes to create a linear graph that could be used to determine Q . Clearly indicate which quantity corresponds to each axis. ii. Briefly describe the relationship between Q and a feature of the graph. Your answer may include an equation that relates Q to this feature of the graph. Another group of students wishes to experimentally determine a numerical value for ε 0 , the electric permittivity of free space. This group has a parallel-plate capacitor in which the separation distance between the plates can be varied. The plates on this capacitor completely overlap one another and have an area A = 0.160 m 2 . With the capacitor connected to a battery of potential difference Δ V = 5.60 V , the students are able to determine the charge Q on the capacitor’s plates for various plate separation distances d . The table shows the results of the experiment. d ( mm Q ( nC ) 2.0 4.13 2.5 3.54 4.0 2.00 7.0 1.14 9.5 0.93 Notes: Plate area A = 0.160 m 2 , Capacitor potential difference Δ V = 5.60 V (c) i. Label the axes of the grid provided with measured or calculated quantities. Include units, as appropriate. The graphed quantities should yield a linear graph that can be used to determine an experimental value for ε 0 . ii. On the grid, create a graph of the quantities indicated in part c (i). q: 103 Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight. A coaxial cylindrical capacitor has a solid central conductor with radius r A and an outer shell with inner radius r B and outer radius r C , as shown in the figure. (a) Indicate how the magnitudes E A and E B of the electric fields at a location r slightly larger than r A and slightly smaller than r B compare to each other by writing one of the following. E A > E B E A < E B E A = E B Justify your selection without manipulating equations or mathematically deriving the selected relation. (b) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the electric potential difference between the inner surface of the shell ( r = r B ) and the surface of the central conductor ( r = r A ) . Express your answer in terms of r A , r B , r C , L , σ A , and physical constants, as appropriate. (c) A second coaxial cylindrical capacitor has the same length as the original capacitor. However, the radii of the second capacitor are r A + Δ r , r B + Δ r , and r C + Δ r for the solid central conductor, the inner radius of the outer shell, and the outer radius of the outer shell, respectively. In other words, each radius of the second capacitor is greater than that of the original capacitor by the same amount Δ r . Indicate how the capacitance C 2 of the second capacitor compares to the capacitance C 1 of the original capacitor by writing one of the following . C 2 > C 1 C 2 < C 1 C 2 = C 1 Use the equation you derived in part (b) to justify your selection.
